466.title: Blackjack
466.synopsis: The casino game everyone thinks they can beat.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Host Ira Glass and producer Robyn Semien get a blackjack lesson from Andy Bloch, who played for the MIT blackjack team. He teaches them the basics of card-counting, the technique that gives players an advantage against the house — enough of an advantage that most casinos will ask you to leave if they catch you doing it.

acts.title: Render Unto Caesar's Palace What Is Due To Caesar's Palace
acts.summary: Jack Hitt tells the story of the Christian card-counting team featured in the documentary Holy Rollers , and why they see no contradiction in being devout Christians who spend their days in casinos.

acts.title: Part Two
acts.summary: Ira and Robyn go to the casino to try out their newfound card-counting skills.

acts.title: Harrah's Today, Gone Tomorrow
acts.summary: Producer Sarah Koenig tells the story of a woman who sued the casino where she lost her inheritance, saying that it was to blame, not her. This story was inspired by a chapter in The Power of Habit , by Charles Duhigg .

acts.title: Act One
acts.summary: The Golden Peacock.

acts.title: Act Four: Potato Potahto
acts.summary: Two people who've nearly died in gun battles describe what it's like, getting shot at.

acts.title: Act Three: The Epiphany Biz
acts.summary: Bill Lychak reports on what it's like to be a factory worker in the Epiphany Plant, bringing news of miracles to Christians in a magazine called Guideposts .

393.title: Infidelity
393.synopsis: Stories of cheating, cheaters, and the cheated.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Ira talks with Jessica Pressler about a phenomenon she noticed in the wedding notices in The New York Times . Couples were cheerfully telling—as part of their "meet cute" stories—how their relationships began with one of them cheating on a spouse or long-time partner.

acts.title: Let Me Kiss Your Stiff Upper Lip
acts.summary: From England, Ruby Wright has a story of an affair where—even years after it ended—it wasn't much discussed.

acts.title: The Italian Job
acts.summary: Ira reviews some infidelity stats from his mother's book on the subject, Not Just Friends . And author James Braly tells a story of temptation live onstage at The Moth .

acts.title: How Did I Get Here?
acts.summary: Dani Shapiro on the confusing mess things can be during an affair. The story is from her memoir, Slow Motion .

acts.title: The Man Who Knew What I Was About To Say
acts.summary: Etgar Keret describes the moment in the immediate aftermath of an affair. Actor Matt Malloy reads.

acts.title: Act Three: Same Time Next Year
acts.summary: Writer/Comedian Amanda Marks shares the story of a couple that didn't fall in love, didn't get together, didn't form a future.

acts.title: Act One: Letters
acts.summary: Ira and playwright David Hauptschein took out advertisements in Chicago inviting people to come to a small theater with letters they've received, sent or found.

acts.title: Act One
acts.summary: In Malawi, in southeast Africa, not gossiping can be worse than gossiping.

885.title: Bless This Mess
885.synopsis: At a time when the U.S. government is trying to make American history tidier, we try to learn from the mess. Including the untold, messy story of Paul and Essie Robeson.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Guest Host Emanuele Berry talks to Nichole Hill about the Black movie characters Nichole was curious about as a child.

acts.title: The Was-It Couple
acts.summary: A giant of the Harlem Renaissance, Paul Robeson was the most famous American of his day. Until he wasn’t. Nichole Hill tells the messy, complicated story of Paul and his wife, Essie Robeson. This story comes from Nichole’s podcast, Our Ancestors Were Messy .

acts.title: Sincerely, Absolutely Not
acts.summary: In 1865, a formerly enslaved man named Jourdan Anderson received a letter from his former enslaver, asking Jourdan to return to the plantation and work. Actor Laurence Fishburne reads Jourdan’s response. This letter was performed in New York City at an event for Letters Live , which stages live readings of famous and remarkable letters in front of a live audience.

acts.title: Act One: Doing Good By Doing Bad
acts.summary: Ron Copeland is a historical interpreter at the Conner Prairie Living History Museum, outside of Indianapolis.

acts.title: Act Two: Killing Me Softly
acts.summary: Producer Emmanuel Dzotsi has a tale about something he avoids at all costs, even though it seems to follow him everywhere he goes.

acts.title: Act Two: Financial Mousetrap
acts.summary: Looked at one way, the current flailing economy is a victim of invention—Wall Street invention.

212.title: The Other Man
212.synopsis: What happens when a new guy comes on the scene and changes the way everyone relates to each other?

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Ira talks with Sarah Koenig about the first and only time a movie star came to her family's house when she was a kid. It didn't go well, for the celebrity or for her. The star was Robert Redford. He arrived and immediately stole all the attention her parents usually lavished on her, their youngest. Worse, they were nervous and strange around him, not themselves at all. Young Sarah was not pleased. Robert Redford paid the price.

acts.title: Psychic Buddha, Qu'est-ce Que C'est
acts.summary: Davy Rothbart's mother is funny, rational, and by most measures, pretty normal. Except that she spends every day in the company of an ancient Buddhist monk named Aaron, who no one else can see. Davy talks to his brothers, father, and eventually his mom, and asks the question they've somehow never managed to discuss: do any of them actually believe he's real? Davy is the man behind Found Magazine.

acts.title: The Jackson Two
acts.summary: Congressman Jesse Jackson, Jr. had always lived in the shadow of his father's name. But just before his primary, an aide delivered strange news: a second Jesse Jackson had appeared on the same ballot — a retired truck driver with no political experience. Ira reports on whether it was a coincidence or mischief orchestrated by the Congressman's rivals.

acts.title: Mr. Fun
acts.summary: Jonathan Goldstein and Heather O'Neill tell the true story of a man trying to wedge himself into an idyllic family of two. For the first few years, Heather's daughter Arizona was not very fond of Jonathan. He ranked nineteenth on her list of favorite people, behind the neighbor's dog and the plumber. Jonathan Goldstein is the author of the book, Lenny Bruce is Dead . Heather O'Neill's is the author of Two Eyes Are You Sleeping , a book of poetry.

acts.title: Act Three: Finding Amnesia
acts.summary: Who among us has not wanted amnesia to help get over someone or something? But the problem with amnesia is that it happens a lot more in TV shows and movies and novels than it does in real life.

acts.title: Act One: Vouching Towards Bethlehem
acts.summary: Secretary of Education Betsy DeVos is from Grand Rapids, Michigan.

acts.title: Act Two: A Picture is Worth A Thousand… Dollars
acts.summary: Painter Schandra Singh usually sells her paintings to wealthy art collectors.

884.title: The Idiot
884.synopsis: M. Gessen returns to our show with a true-crime story that takes place entirely within their own family. This story comes to us from the producers atSerial Productions—who invented the true-crime podcast more than a decade ago—and fromThe New York Times.

acts.title: Act One
acts.summary: M Gessen tells Ira Glass about the surprising events that prompted them to begin reporting on their own family for their new podcast, The Idiot . They play the first episode of the series.

acts.title: Act Two
acts.summary: Ira Glass and M Gessen continue to talk through the story of M’s cousin, Allen Gessen. They play more clips from the podcast, and we finally hear about the big, shocking thing that snapped their family apart.

acts.title: Act Three
acts.summary: M Gessen tells Ira Glass about Allen’s trial, and we hear a recording of his conversation with the undercover agent.

acts.title: Act Five: What Peacetime Forgets About Wartime
acts.summary: A few years back, a writer named Lee Sandlin wrote a story for the weekly paper The Chicago Reader about what makes wartime different—how a country's perceptions and logic during war are fundamentally different than during peace.

acts.title: Act One: Orange You Glad I Didn’t Say Banana?
acts.summary: Adam Davidson and Chana Joffe-Walt from Planet Money head to the Hunts Point Market in the Bronx, a bustling area of vegetable and fruit commerce that only comes alive at night.

acts.title: Act Three: It Had to Be YouTube
acts.summary: For the last five years or so, Johnny Dark has kept up a very specific, unusual, and labor intensive daily practice.

883.title: Call Your Parents
883.synopsis: In the early days of the radio show, Ira did a series of interviews with his parents that completely changed his relationship with them. This week, he returns to those interviews.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Ira talks about why four conversations reveal how his relationship with his parents changed.

acts.title: Interview One
acts.summary: Ira’s mom, Shirley, is invited to lead a discussion about how to get along with your adult children. Her adult children question her expertise.

acts.title: Interview Two
acts.summary: Ira asks his parents for advice on how he should build the radio show. His parents don’t hold back.

acts.title: Interview Three
acts.summary: Ira talks with his dad, Barry, about Barry’s own brief and doomed career in radio.

acts.title: Interview Four
acts.summary: An interview with Ira’s mom that, to this day, makes Ira’s skin crawl.

acts.title: Act Three: Reverb
acts.summary: Ellery Eskelin never met his father but always heard he was a musical genius.

acts.title: Act One: Roadrunner
acts.summary: Ira teaches Sarah Vowell how to drive with some advice from Tom and Ray Maggliozi, the hosts of NPR's Car Talk. It turns out that although we think of how-to's as the most rational thing in the world — follow the simple instructions and you'll learn — in real life, they're anything but simple.

acts.title: Act Two: No One Has Seen Them Made or Heard Them Made
acts.summary: Ira Glass talks with Robert Costa of The Washington Post about President Trump's promise that Mexico would build the wall.

882.title: Give a Little Whistle
882.synopsis: Two lawyers who work for ICE step forward and lift the curtain on what is really happening inside our immigration system right now.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Two lawyers dive into the details of what they’ve witnessed behind the scenes in different parts of the immigration system.

acts.title: I Got the Memo
acts.summary: Former ICE attorney Ryan Schwank explains the chaos and dysfunction he observed at an ICE training academy, which led him to whistleblow to Congress two weeks ago.

acts.title: Don’t Trust the Process
acts.summary: A federal judge orders the government to immediately release a bunch of people from detention. Days pass, and the government doesn’t comply. So the judge calls a hearing to figure out what’s going on. The lawyer's response is not what he or anybody expected.

acts.title: Act Nine: Salt Lake City
acts.summary: Elna Baker talks to a Republican woman who voted for Hillary Clinton this year, and has suffered consequences in the Mormon community she lives in.

acts.title: Act Three: Pigeons on a Plane
acts.summary: Reporter Kevin Sieff travels from Mexico to Chicago with a group of seniors reuiniting with their undocumented kids in the U.S., some for the first time in decades.

acts.title: Act Two: Chicken Diva
acts.summary: Yet another testimony to the power chickens have over our hearts and minds.  Jack Hitt reports on an opera about Chicken Little.  It's performed with dressed-up styrofoam balls, it's sung in Italian and, no kidding, able to make grown men cry.The official website for the opera "Love's Fowl" by Susan Vitucci and Henry Krieger is pulcina.org.

881.title: I Want What I Want
881.synopsis: People deciding to do things that most of us do NOT choose to do.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: A new documentary called The Boys and the Bees captures a moment where a six-year-old has a very unlikely wish, and his dad decides to grant it. Host Ira Glass talks with filmmaker Arielle Knight about what happens next. Arielle Knight is an award-winning director and producer of The Boys and the Bees . The film is currently touring festivals worldwide and will air on POV on PBS in the fall of 2026.

acts.title: Lessons in Shellfishness
acts.summary: John Tothill tells the story of Edward Dando, a 19th-century British glutton who would eat hundreds of oysters at a time and then run out on the check. John makes the case that we should all be more like Edward Dando. John’s show was directed by Alex Cartlidge and Kathryn Craigmyle. He’s on tour across the UK right now, details at @johntothill .

acts.title: The Joy of Ex
acts.summary: Producer Tobin Low listens in as Evan Roberts calls up an ex for the first time in years and tries to make the case that they should have been friends all along. Evan wrote about his conversations with all his exes on his Substack, Don't Be Precious, Darling .

acts.title: The Lawyer, The Judge and The Wardrobe
acts.summary: Producer Zoe Chace brings us a dispatch from a courtroom in Texas this week, where on the very first day of a landmark federal trial about Antifa, the judge makes an unusual decision that no one sees coming.

acts.title: Act One: Christmas On A High Wire
acts.summary: Some of the best improv actors in the country join us for a special Christmas themed performance recorded live at the Bellhouse in Brooklyn.

acts.title: Act One: Best Laid Plans
acts.summary: Kurt Braunohler and his girlfriend had been together for thirteen years, and they were only 30.

acts.title: Act Two: More Powerful Than A Locomotive
acts.summary: One of the most powerful forces in a room can be the thing that is unspoken between people.

880.title: What Is Your Emergency?
880.synopsis: 911 calls unlike any we’ve heard before, and other stories about immigration agents sweeping through America.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: A collection of 911 calls where you can hear immigration enforcement moving through different cities and leaving chaos in their wake.

acts.title: What’s the Nature of Your Emergency?
acts.summary: More 911 calls, including people on the line with dispatchers as ICE is chasing them, trying to puzzle out their next moves. Ryan Oehrli and Julia Coin collected many of these 911 calls for The Charlotte Observer , and Jim Daley for Southside Weekly in Chicago.

acts.title: There’s No Place like Home…Depot
acts.summary: Home Depots keep getting raided over and over again in Los Angeles. And day laborers are still showing up in store parking lots to find work every day.  So what’s that like? Months and months of that cat and mouse? Anayansi Diaz-Cortes went to find out.

acts.title: Memo to the Future
acts.summary: Memo Torres tries to build an archive of every person taken by federal agents in Southern California. L.A. Taco's " The Daily Memo "

acts.title: Act One: White Like Me
acts.summary: David Rakoff on how he tried to pass as a local once he moved from Toronto to New York.

acts.title: Act One: Run On Sentence
acts.summary: Lots of us have things about us that we keep to ourselves.

acts.title: Act Two: Who Tells Your Story?
acts.summary: For years Pat Buchanan ran on many of the same ideas that Donald Trump would later run on.

879.title: A Christian and a Muslim Walk Into a Bar
879.synopsis: When a joke could get you killed, should you say it anyway? A group of Syrian comedians test the limits of their newfound freedom, a year after the fall of the brutal Assad regime.

acts.title: Prologue
acts.summary: Under the dictatorship of Bashar al-Assad, comedian Sharief Homsi knew which jokes were too dangerous to say on stage. Now that Syria is under the control of a new government, Sharief and the other comedians of “Styria” set out on a national tour to see how far their comedy can go in this new Syria.

acts.title: Too Soon?
acts.summary: The comedians test out risky material and get big laughs on early tour dates. It’s going smoothly until they find out that their show scheduled in the conservative city of Hama is in danger of being cancelled.

acts.title: The Drama in Hama
acts.summary: The comedians go to battle with local officials.

acts.title: Is This Thing On?
acts.summary: The comedians try everything they can think of to keep their shows from being cancelled.

acts.title: Act Two: Romancing the Phone
acts.summary: Comedian Aziz Ansari has been touring the country collecting people’s text messages from when they first say hi, and ask each other out.

acts.title: Act Two
acts.summary: We continue our story about three members of Warriors in the Garden. After a summer of protest, the Warriors have to figure out what to do when their activism draws the attention of the police.

acts.title: Act One: Confession
acts.summary: Patrick Wall was a special kind of monk.
